text: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are believed to be suitable candidate materials for low-temperature heat transformation applications, especially in thermally driven adsorption chillers and heat pumps. Comparing with conventional adsorbents like silica gels and zeolites, MOFs have outstanding microporosity and surface properties which can be controlled by changing different components in MOFs. Moreover, superior water uptake capacity and selectivity towards specific components can be attained by designing the frameworks to be highly hydrophobic or hydrophilic according to the desired working condition. Therefore, low-grade solar energy as well as industrial waste heat could be efficiently utilized by using the MOF-based system instead of consuming plenty of electrical energy in traditional systems. In this review, an overview of MOF materials in heat transformation application is provided. Compared performances of MOFs with conventional materials and their corresponding stabilities after hydrothermal treatments are summarized. Effects of geometrical structures and coatings or treatments on the thermal conductivity and mass transport of MOFs are discussed as well.
explanation: The text is coherent and uses domain-specific terminology typical of reviews. The slightly generic phrasing and minor awkward grammar patterns suggest it could be AI-generated, though it's also plausible as human-authored scientific prose.
